author: @kennybentley1161
text:
hey rob, I was thinking about other useful capacitor designs, and I don't know if this is feasible or now, but if a capacitor could be designed such that it had a linear discharge curve, then that capacitor could be extremely useful for DAC signal smoothing. for example, imagine a piece of audio equipment like an mp3 player or something. the output of that signal (because it's digital) comes out looking like a stair steps for a sinusoidal wave. usually a capacitor is used to smooth out those steps, but as you know, a capacitor voltage drops exponentially the closer it gets to zero. what this means is not a smooth signal output and the signal noticeably lags behind. so, a capacitor with a linear discharge would be huge for anyone wanting to do high quality signal smoothing for cheap. there are so many uses that this could have.


so, I was thinking about how this could possibly be done, and I was reminded of your "strange capacitor" video where you explored capacitors with a high dielectric v-value for the separator. if there could be a material that reduces its k-value as charge decreases, then a more linear discharge curve could be achieved.
!
anyway, it was just a thought that occurred to me the night before last and I wanted to share it with you. your work is amazing, peace :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: interesting thought mate - I'll ponder it - cheers

author: @4gauge10
text:
This same technology is used on the Japanese bullet trains Robert,the entire train"rides"on electro-magnets,for a ride that's unparalleled anywhere in the world.
I watched a video of a glass of🍷wine sitting on a table and they had a camera close up of it,at well over 300 miles per hour,the wine didn't move not one millimeter inside of that glass.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: been on it mate - it's awesome

replies.author: @4gauge10
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Cool,I've been wanting to try it every since I watched how smooth it rides.
